content: I've been completely overhauling my daily routine since I switched careers from journalism to being a chef. Now I own a small restaurant in Portland.
That's quite a career change! What prompted the switch to culinary arts?
I've always loved cooking, but I finally took the leap last year. My mornings now start at 5 AM with checking on my beekeeping setup before heading to the restaurant.
Beekeeping sounds fascinating! How did you get into that?
I started with two hives three years ago and now I have six. The honey goes into some of my signature dishes. I also spend weekends foraging for mushrooms in the forests around Mt. Hood.
Foraging sounds adventurous! What kind of mushrooms do you typically find?
Mostly chanterelles and morels when they're in season. I use them in my tasting menu. After the morning prep, I teach a cooking class at 11, then lunch service starts at 1.
You're certainly keeping busy! What do you teach in your cooking classes?

I focus on farm-to-table techniques and preserving seasonal ingredients. I'm 48, so I've got a lot of experience to share. In the evenings, I unwind by reading philosophy - right now I'm on my third reading of 'Being and Time.'
Heidegger is quite dense! Do you find philosophy helps with your creative process in the kitchen?
Absolutely. I think about concepts like authenticity a lot when developing new dishes. I also picked up salsa dancing last year - it's a great stress reliever after a long day in the kitchen.
Salsa dancing sounds like a fun way to stay active. Do you dance regularly?
I go to classes twice a week at a studio downtown. I'm still pretty clumsy, but I'm improving. My garden at home is another passion - I grow most of the herbs for the restaurant myself.
